name: Gene Austin
abstract: Lemeul Eugene Lucas (June 24, 1900 – January 24, 1972), better known by his stage name Gene Austin, was an American singer and songwriter, one of the early "crooners". His recording of "My Blue Heaven" sold over 5 million copies and was for a while the largest selling record of all time.

works_created.t: How Come You Do Me Like You Do?

about_quotes.speaker: David Albert Francis
about_quotes.evidence: Gene Austin -- he came to Miami I think in 1926, and had a big turnout there for him, you know?

about_quotes.speaker: Arthur "Monk" Hazel
about_quotes.evidence: I went to Hollywood with Gene Austin... We only had four pieces... And three times Austin would sing a couple of songs with us.

about_quotes.speaker: Jon Hendricks
about_quotes.evidence: I took after Gene Austin. I liked Gene Austin. He was on the radio every night for half an hour. He had a high tenor voice like I had. So I used to sing a lot of his songs.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jon Hendricks — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Cliff Leeman
about_quotes.evidence: you have been involved with Basie, in Kansas City with Dave Tough, Jo Jones, Gene Austin Jim Krupa, with Baby Dodds, Buddy Rich and Cubie? -- Sid Catlett

about_quotes.speaker: Buddy Tate
about_quotes.evidence: Then there were a lot of blacks even turned on to Gene Austin, who -- I think he sold more -- nobody's ever topped his "Blue Heaven," I don't think.

about_quotes.speaker: Jeff Riddick
about_quotes.evidence: Yes, I knew Gene Austin... Well, I met him when he was here, playing, and when Monk was playing with him and Coco and Candy.
